coredata.dc:title: Electrical synaptic devices with a high recognition rate based on eco-friendly nanocomposites of a poly(methyl methacrylate) matrix embedded with graphene quantum dots for neuromorphic computing
coredata.dc:creator: Ryu, Seong Yeon
Kim, Hyung Soon
An, Jun Seop
Kim, Youngjin
An, Haoqun
Kim, Jong-Ryeol
Yoon, Kijung
Kim, Tae Whan
coredata.dc:description: Artificial synapse devices are currently the subjects of great attention as next-generation hardware for data processing to overcome the problem of data explosion due to the rapid advances in artificial intelligence and cloud computing technology. Nanocomposite-based devices enable unique applications and have several advantages that cannot be achieved in single material-based devices. This study presents binary electrical synapses with digital data storing and analog data processing through a nanocomposite-based active layer composed of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) with embedded chlorine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (fGQDs) on an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate. The Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO devices with an fGQD concentrations of 5 wt% exhibited excellent memory performance with RON/ROFF ratio of 103. Moreover, we demonstrated that our device can successfully emulate biological synaptic functions such as potentiation/depression, short-term/long-term memory, paired-pulse facilitation, learning experience, and spike-timing-dependent plasticity. Furthermore, on the basis of the synaptic behaviors of the devices, they achieved about a 90 % recognition capability when a learning algorithm was used in a single-layer neural network.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Resistive random access memory
Artificial synaptic device
Neuromorphic computing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean government ( MSIT ) (No. 2018R1A5A7025522 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design and characterization. (a) Structure diagrams and (b) focused ion beam scanning electron microscopy (FIB-SEM) image of Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO synaptic device. (c) TEM image of the fGQDs (scale bar: 10 nm) and the corresponding high-resolution TEM image (scale bar: 2 nm). (d) Diameter distribution histogram of the fGQDs and its (e) X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS), and their high-resolution (f) C1s XPS spectra.

Memristive performance of the Ag/PMMA-fGQD/ITO device. (a) I–V characteristics and bipolar switching behavior of the devices with different fGQD concentrations. (b) Double logarithmic plotting of the HRS and the LRS for a negative voltage sweep. (c) Schematic energy band diagrams of the carrier transport mechanisms (c) in an equilibrium state, (d) at low negative voltages, and (e) at high negative voltages sufficient to fill trap states.

Synaptic characteristics of the device. (a) The schematic illustration of the similarity between the biological neurons and the resistive random access memory (RRAM) device. (b) Paired-pulse facilitation (PPF) of the device as a function of the time interval between two pulses. (c) Synaptic potentiation and synaptic depression by electrical pulse stimulation. (d) Energy consumption required per applied pulse used during the potentiation and the depression measurements. (e) Normalized current after different numbers of pulses (STM-to-LTM). (f) Memory retention and relaxation time (τ) as functions of the number of pulses. (g) Dependence of the synaptic weight on the spike time in STDP.

Learning experience of the memristive device. (a) Learning, forgetting, and relearning the behaviors of the Ag/PMMA-fGQD/ITO device. (b) Seven kinds of devices are used to form the shape of the letter “H”; the shade of the color of the “H” represents the retention level of the learning experience. (c) Variation in the synaptic weight triggered by two different initial pulses.

MNIST-based simulation method and results. (a) Schematic diagram of the digital “6” recognition process using a single-layer artificial neural network. (b) The recognition rate of the MNIST pattern for both the Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO-based experimental case and the software-based numerical case. (c) Variations in the distribution of the normalized weight within the trained neural network by using the number of epochs. (d) Weight map of the integers from 0 to 9 after the 0th, 1st, and 50th training epochs based on the synaptic weight of the synaptic device. (e) Confusion matrix of the neural network after learning and inference tests at the 0th, 1st, and 50th epochs.

head.ce:title: Electrical synaptic devices with a high recognition rate based on eco-friendly nanocomposites of a poly(methyl methacrylate) matrix embedded with graphene quantum dots for neuromorphic computing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Industrial Science, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Industrial Science, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Sejong University, 209, Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul, 05006, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, Sejong University, 209, Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul 05006, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Artificial synapse devices are currently the subjects of great attention as next-generation hardware for data processing to overcome the problem of data explosion due to the rapid advances in artificial intelligence and cloud computing technology. Nanocomposite-based devices enable unique applications and have several advantages that cannot be achieved in single material-based devices. This study presents binary electrical synapses with digital data storing and analog data processing through a nanocomposite-based active layer composed of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) with embedded chlorine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (fGQDs) on an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate. The Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO devices with an fGQD concentrations of 5 wt% exhibited excellent memory performance with RON/ROFF ratio of 103. Moreover, we demonstrated that our device can successfully emulate biological synaptic functions such as potentiation/depression, short-term/long-term memory, paired-pulse facilitation, learning experience, and spike-timing-dependent plasticity. Furthermore, on the basis of the synaptic behaviors of the devices, they achieved about a 90 % recognition capability when a learning algorithm was used in a single-layer neural network.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Memristive devices have attracted great attention for achieving hardware implementation of a neuromorphic computing system because they are suitable in neuromorphic architecture for synaptic devices due to their low power consumption and fast processing time [1–5]. Synaptic devices refer to memory devices that can operate by mimicking various synaptic behaviors in a biological neural network, such as long-term memory (LTM), short-term memory (STM), learning-forgetting and relearning, and spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) [6–8]. Synaptic devices based on memristors have the great advantage of being able to operate at very low power while excellently mimicking these synaptic behaviors [9].

For this reason, extensive efforts have been made to integrate neuromorphic hardware systems based on memristors for applications in artificial intelligence systems and for various other applications, such as computer science, biology, etc [10,11]. However, various physical characteristics, such as non-toxicity and high efficiency are essential characteristics if the materials are to be used in devices with promising applications in neuromorphic computing systems. In this regard, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently attracted attention due to their being a non-toxic, environmentally friendly material, and they have replaced toxic heavy metal materials such as the CdSe QDs or CdZnS QDs, which previously had been extensively investigated for conventional memristive devices [12–14]. In addition, because the quantum confinement effect and the edge effect are significant in small QDs below 10 nm; and because QD materials can offer sufficient numbers of trapping states, the performances of these memristive devices have improved [15–17]. Currently, a lot of research on QD-embedded polymer-based memristor has attracted great attention, since QDs can act as trapping centers within the active layer, and they have attention as excellent alternatives to oxide-based active layers [18]. For example, Sahu et al. reported an ultralow-power RRAM device with an Al/CdSe-PVP/Al structure [19]. Tang et al. also proposed a method embedding carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in PMMA for a high-performance RRAM device with an Ag/PMMA&CQDs/FTO structure [20]. For these reasons, and because GQDs exhibit excellent electrical characteristics, they have attracted attention for potential applications in nonvolatile memory devices [21,22]. In recent years, Cristea et al. reported the effect of embedding GQDs in P3HT [23]. They also verified that GQDs act as trapping centers in polymer-based memristors and that GQDs can greatly enhance the charge transport characteristics [24]. However, GQD applications in memory devices are restricted due to the limitations of pure GQDs [25]. For example, the intrinsic hydrophilicity of GQDs can cause nanoparticle aggregation in the polymer matrix and make fabricating a high-quality nanocomposite difficult [26,27]. To prevent such inherent problems, researchers can use chemical functionalization to reduce the aggregation of the nanoparticles and increase the aqueous solubility by modifying the surface chemistry of the GQDs with heteroatoms like Cl atoms [28,29]. Therefore, the functionalization of GQDs with an appropriate material can effectively increase their dispersibility in a polymer matrix, resulting in improved stability and electrical properties for the memristive devices [30].

In this study, the electrical properties of Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO artificial synaptic devices fabricated utilizing fGQDs and PMMA were investigated, and the electrical characteristics of low operation voltage, high on/off ratio, and reliable resistance switching were clarified. We also investigated various biological synaptic functions, such as paired-pulse facilitation (PPF), long-term potentiation (LTP), long-term depression (LTD), transition from the STM to LTM, learning experience, and STDP. We used the Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology (MNIST) pattern recognition algorithm for simulation based on the conductance of the devices and found that a 90 % recognition rate could be achieved from a single-layer neural network.

We purchased fGQDs from ACS Material (CAS NO 7440-40-0). We then used them to fabricate synaptic devices with an Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO structure. The ITO/glass was cleaned ultrasonically in acetone, methanol, and de-ionized (DI) water for 20 min each and dried with N2 gas. After the cleaning process, the surface of the ITO glass was treated with ultraviolet (UV)-ozone cleaner for 20 min. PMMA was dissolved in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) by using an ultrasonic process for 8 h at 40 °C. The fGQD solution was added to the PMMA-DMF solution in a weight ratio of 1, 5, or 10 wt%, followed by magnetic stirring for 8 h. The PMMA-fGQD thin layer was deposited on ITO/glass by using a spin-coating method. After the PMMA-fGQD film had been spin-coated on the cleaned ITO substrate at an average of 2500 rpm for 30 s, the devices were annealed at about 150 °C for 40 min. An Al electrode with a thickness of about 200 nm and a diameter of 500 μm was deposited on the PMMA-fGQD layer by using a thermal evaporator at a system pressure of 2 × 10−5 Torr.

The electrical properties of the Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO device were analyzed by using a Keithley 4200 semiconductor characterization system (SCS) and a Keithley 4255-PMU dual channel current-voltage (I–V) unit. The structural and chemical properties of our devices were measured by using focused ion beam-scanning electron microscopy (FIB-SEM, Thermofisher Helios G5 UC), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-4010), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermofisher Nexsa).

A schematic diagram of the memristive device with an Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO structure is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The cross-sectional FIB-SEM image of the device is presented in Fig. 1b, which shows that the active layer is uniformly spin-coated and distributed with a thickness of about 65 nm. Fig. 1c shows the TEM images of the fGQDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, fGQDs with diameters of 1∼3.5 nm are dispersed without any aggregation, and the lattice spacing of the (100) planes for the graphene is approximately 0.24 nm. The particle size distribution of the fGQDs can be obtained from the TEM image, and Fig. 1d represents the histogram for the diameter distribution of the fGQD nanoparticles, and their average size is calculated as 2.0664 ± 0.6321 nm. Fig. 1e shows the results of an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis for the fGQDs. As shown in the XPS survey spectrum, the fGQDs have a component of C, and intense C1s peaks are located at 286 and 534 eV, respectively. The spectrum of C1s can be deconvoluted into four surface components: CC (284.6 eV), C–Cl/C–OH (286.5 eV), C–O–C (287.3 eV), and CO (288.7 eV) (Fig. 1f) [31,32].

To investigate the effect of the fGQD concentration on the electrical characteristics of the devices, we measured the I–V characteristics of four devices, each with a different fGQD concentration. Fig. 2 a shows the I–V characteristics of the Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO structure with fGQD concentrations of 1, 5, or 10 wt%. To prevent the permanent breakdown of the device, we set the compliance current at 0.1 A. When a DC voltage sweep was applied to the device with an fGQD concentration of 1 wt% (0 → −1.3 (OFF) → −3.0 (ON) → 2.6 (ON) → 3 (OFF) → 0), the I–V characteristic of the device showed a resistive switching characteristic with a low ON/OFF ratio of approximately 10. When the DC voltage sweep was applied to the device with an fGQD concentration of 5 wt% (0 → −1.4 (OFF) → −3.0 (ON) → 1.0 (ON) → 3 (OFF) → 0), the currents in the ON and the OFF states at −1 V were 3.95
×
10−5 and 4.75
×
10−2 A, respectively. On the other hand, when the DC voltage sweep was applied to the device with an fGQD concentration of 10 wt% (0 → −1.8(OFF) → −3.0(ON) → 1.8(ON) → 3(OFF) → 0), the currents of the ON and the OFF states at −1 V were 2.87
×
10−4 and 4.23
×
10−2 A, respectively. Because an increased concentration of fGQDs up to 10 wt% generates excessive trap states, additional voltage is required to fill and empty those states, causing the SET/RESET voltages to be increased. Even though the memristive switching behaviors were observed in both devices, one with a fGQD concentration of 5 and the other with a fGQD of 10 wt%, the ON/OFF ratio of the former device was approximately 8.2 times larger than that of the latter device. We observed that the conductivity of the device increased with increasing fGQD concentration and that the current in the high resistance state was relatively large, resulting in a decrease in the value of the ON/OFF ratio. For a comparison of our devices with other PMMA-based memristors, some electrical performances of various PMMA-based RRAM devices are summarized in Table S1. To investigate the conduction mechanism of the SET operation for the Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO device with an fGQD concentration of 5 wt%, we plotted a double logarithmic I–V curve in the negative voltage sweep region. Because QDs can act as electron trapping centers, the trap-controlled mechanism and the formation of the space-charge electric field will dominate the general conduction process [33,34]. However, slight differences can be seen depending on the concentration of QDs, in which the I–V curves for different fGQD concentrations were plotted on a logarithmic scale in Fig. S5.

As shown in Fig. 2b, the I–V curves can be divided into three regions with distinct slopes: black, blue, and red. For the black (0–0.7 V) and the blue regions, the slopes are approximately equal to 1, indicating that Ohmic conduction is the dominant transport mechanism. Especially in blue regions, ohmic conduction was observed due to the well-formed conducting filaments in the LRS due to the trapped electrons [33]. On the other hand, according to Child’s law, space charge limited current (SCLC) is the dominant transport mechanism in the red region (0.8–1.4 V), where the slope is approximately equal to 2 [35,36]. The carrier transport mechanisms of the high resistance state (HRS) to the low resistance state (LRS) can be investigated by using the energy band diagrams of the Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO device (Fig. 2c–e). The energy band diagram of the device in an equilibrium state is illustrated in Fig. 2c. Because the energy level of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs), the fGQDs is lower than that of the PMMA (−1.8 eV from the vacuum level), the fGQDs can act as trapping centers in the charge transport process [37,38]. When electrons injected from the Al electrode move into the active layer due to an applied bias voltage, the trapping centers within the active layer become filled with electrons. Consequently, the energy level of the fGQDs within the polymer increases, and the formation of a space-charge electric field becomes the dominant conduction process [33]. Band bending might occur when a negative voltage is applied to the Al electrode, as shown in Fig. 2d. Due to the trap states created by the fGQDs, only a few hot electrons can be transferred from the Al to the ITO electrodes, resulting in a high-resistance state (HRS). When the voltage is high enough to fill all trap states, the SCLC process occurs. Fig. 2e shows that the trap states are full at a high negative voltage. As a result, when the trapping centers are completely full, the Fermi level of the fGQDs increases up to the LUMO level of PMMA and electrons can be easily transferred from the Al to the ITO electrodes without the effects of trapping. Thus, the current through the device dramatically increases because of the conductive filaments that are formed in the PMMA-fGQD layer [33]. In this case, because the electrons can be transferred from the Al to the ITO electrodes without the effects of trapping, resulting in a low-resistance state (LRS), the current through the device dramatically increases.

Because the memristor can mimic various biological synaptic behaviors, our device is suitable for application in artificial synapses. Fig. 3 a is a schematic diagram showing the similarities between biological neurons and memristive devices. Biologically, the information can be transferred from one neuron to another via electrical synaptic transmission with electrical signals or via chemical synaptic transmission, in which neurotransmitters released from a pre-neuron are transferred to a post-neuron [39]. The number of absorbed or released neurotransmitters is closely related to the synaptic weight, which refers to the synaptic strength between two neurons and corresponds to the value of the conductance in memristive devices [40–42]. Synaptic weight based on Hebbian learning rules can be changed depending on the activity of two neurons by using a spike signal [43]. The characteristics of the variations for the synaptic weight are referred to as synaptic plasticity and their behaviors can be mimicked in a memristive device [44]. Here, we have investigated various synaptic behaviors, such as LTP, LTD, STM, LTM, PPF, learning experience, and STDP, of the Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO devices. The PPF refers to synaptic behavior, during which the excitatory postsynaptic current (EPSC) changes with the interval time between two successive stimuli at each adjacent neuron [45]. The PPF value is defined as the ratio of the height of the second peak (A1) to that of the first one (A2) and Δt is the interval time between A1 and A2 [46]. Fig. 3b shows the measured PPF value as a function of Δt. When Δt is very small, the second pulse quickly reaches its maximum before the first ESPC peak has time to sufficiently decrease and the PPF increases. Therefore, the PPF value exponentially decreases with increasing Δt, and the extracted relaxation time constants τ
1 and τ
2 are 2.96 and 301.67 ms, respectively. Fig. 3c shows the variation in the conductance of the memristive device when an electric pulse corresponding to the spike signal in a bio-synapse is applied to the device. One hundred negative pulses (−1.2 V, 2.5 ms) and 100 positive pulses (+0.9 V, 2.5 ms) per cycle were applied over 10 cycles, and a read voltage (+0.1 V, 2.5 ms) was applied between each pulse to observe potentiation and depression. While the conductance value after the 1st negative pulse was 3.63 mS, after the 100th negative pulse it had increased nonlinearly to 3.95 mS. After the 101th and the 200th positive pulses, the conductance values decreased to 3.77 and 3.45 mS, respectively. For effective hardware implementation of neural networks, one of the most important characteristics required of a synaptic device is low power consumption. A huge amount of energy is required to update the weights in the artificial neural network (ANN), that is, to change the conductance of the artificial synapse in the programmable memristor-based ANN hardware. The energy consumed per pulse in the synaptic device during LTP and LTD operations can be obtained by multiplying the amplitude of the read voltage, output current of the read voltage, and width of the read voltage pulse.

Fig. 3d shows that the required active energy of a synaptic device per single-read pulse ranges from 86.2 to 99.0 pJ for one cycle of LTP and LTD. The average power dissipation of 92.8 pJ for our devices is approximately ten times lower than that of CMOS-based artificial synaptic circuits [47]. The synaptic device with an Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO structure can mimic biological characteristics, such as the biological memory process model proposed by Atkinson and Shiffrin [48]. According to their model, the information in the STM state can be transferred to the LTM state through rehearsal of the STM. Once the information is transferred to the LTM, the memory lasts longer than the STM, i.e., the memory retention time of the LTM is increased [49]. Fig. 3e shows the normalized current for about 200 s after applying N (N = 40, 60, 80, and 100) programming pulses (−1.2 V, 2.5 ms). When N = 40, the normalized current value at 200 s is dramatically decreased by 15.11 % compared to its initial value, which corresponds to the forgetting phenomenon of the STM caused by using an insufficient number of repeated rehearsals. As the number of pulses is gradually increased, the rate of variation in the normalized current decreases. When N = 60 and 80, the normalized current after 200 s has increased by 44.18 and 74.32 % compared to their initial values, respectively. When N = 100, the memory retention after 200 s is about 94.19 % without memory degradation, which corresponds to the LTM with a sufficient number of repeated rehearsals. As shown in Fig. 3f, memory retention increases with increasing number of applied pulses. Moreover, the relaxation time also increases with the increasing number of pulses, indicating that the tendency of the variation from STM into LTM increases and that the memory time becomes longer [50]. The relaxation time dependence of the number of pulses can be fitted if equation M(t) = Me + A[exp(-t/τ)] is used, where M(t) is the memory retention level, Me and A are constant values, and τ is the memory relaxation time [51]. The STDP characteristic of the synaptic device was investigated, and the results are presented in Fig. 3g. According to the STDP learning rule, the weight variation (Δw) is affected by the timing of the spike (Δt), which is the relative time difference between the spike signals from the pre-and the post-neurons [52]. If the pre-spike precedes the post-spike, Δt is positive; and vice versa, Δt is negative. In the device, the pre-neuron and the post-neuron correspond to the top electrode and the bottom electrode, respectively. The STDP curves are fitted to the following equation:
(1)
Δ W =
{
A
+
exp
( −
|
Δ t
|
/ τ
+
)
+ Δ
W
0 +
(

>
0
)
A
‐
exp
( −
|
Δ t
|

‐
)
+ Δ
W
0 ‐
(
Δ t
<
0
)
where A ±, τ ±, and ΔW
0± are scaling factors, the time constants, and the values of Δw for very large Δt [53]. The ratios A + to A - and τ + to τ - are 90.11/-152.22 and −15.19/17.12, respectively. When the values of Δt are 5 and -5 ms, the values of ΔW are −96.55 and 55.25 %, respectively.

Even though some challenges, such as reliability and complexity in hardware implementation based on-chip learning, remain our device has the advantage of excellent data security and energy efficiency because the data are stored only in nonvolatile memory devices during the training process [54]. In this regard, understanding the learning experience behavior of synaptic devices is important for achieving reliable neural networks. Fig. 4 a shows the learning experience behavior of our Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO device. The initial normalized synaptic weight of the device is increased from 0 to 100 % by using 40 consecutive pulses (−1.2 V, 2.5 ms), which corresponds to the “learning” process. After a relaxation time of 15 s, the memory retention time has decreased to 33.13 %, which corresponds to a ‘forgetting’’ process in biological synapses. After the “forgetting1” process, 28 consecutive pulses are applied to the device, and the memory retention increases to approximately 97.9 %. After the same relaxation time of 15 s, the memory retention decreased to 47.15 % (forgetting2), which is 14.02 % less than the memory loss in the “forgetting1” process. After the “forgetting2” process, only 20 consecutive pulses have to be applied to achieve 100 % memory retention. After the “forgetting3” process, the memory retention has decreased to only 67.25 % after 15 s. We provide intuitive insight into the learning-forgetting-relearning process by using a letter ‘H’-shaped ‘3
×
3’ lattice and seven devices, as shown in Fig. 4b. The brightness of each cell represents the memory retention of the device, and the initial memory retention is normalized to 110 %. When the initial stimulation is applied 60 times, the average memory retention time of the seven devices decreases to 33.48 % after a forgetting time of 15 s. The average memory retention time has increased to 100.54 and 100.66 % after 28 and 20 consecutive pulses, respectively. During the “forgetting1” and “forgetting2” processes, the average memory retention decreases to 45.06 and 66.67 %, respectively. Fig. 4c shows another learning experience behavior. The initial stimulation pulses (−1.2 V, 2.5 ms) are applied 40 or 60 times to the device, respectively. According to the principle underlying LTM and the STM (Fig. 3f), the higher the number of repetitions in the learning process is, the higher the memory retention time that can be maintained. Therefore, if the applied initial stimulation process is sufficient (N = 60), the relearning process should require a smaller number of iterations than is required for an initial stimulation that is less sufficient (N = 40). As a result, when the initial condition is N = 40 pulses, 28 pulses are required for the relearning process after a relaxation time of 15 s. On the other hand, when the initial condition is N = 60 pulses, only 22 pulses are required for the relearning process after the same relaxation time. The above experimental results verify that our Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO-based memristor can mimic various biotic synaptic functions, which is indicative of a synaptic device with exceptionable performance.

Based on the mentioned synaptic functions, synaptic devices for hardware applications can be used to synapse in a neural network. Therefore, we evaluated the Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology (MNIST) pattern recognition for software simulation based on the data from the LTP and the LTD characteristics of the synaptic devices in the learning framework. As Fig. 5 a illustrates, the single layer-neural network used in this simulation consists of an input layer of 784 neurons and an output layer of 10 neurons. Each neuron is connected to the others by 7840 nodes, which correspond to hardware applications using an electrical memristor as an artificial synapse [55]. However, for the integration of the high-density crossbar array architecture based on two-terminal memristors, the leakage current and crosstalk generated between each cell must be resolved. Therefore, synaptic transistors have drawn attention to the practical realizations of high-density neuromorphic computing applications [56].

When the pixel value of the image corresponding to “6” in the MINST dataset is introduced to the input layer, the output pixel value “6” is obtained from the neural network. In our single-layer neural network, the activation function and the error function are rectified linear unit (ReLU) and a cross-entropy loss function, respectively, and the softmax function is used for processing the output signal. The values of the weights for the neural network are updated using the backpropagation and the gradient-descent methods, and the new value of the weight is adjusted to an ideal value in each epoch. When the learning rate (α) is too small to converge slowly to a moderate value, the weight is updated at a very slow rate, and a very long time is required to attain a high recognition rate. On the other hand, when the learning rate is too large in comparison with the experimentally expected magnitude, the weight does not converge to the ideal value, resulting in a divergent weight. Therefore, the setting of an appropriate learning rate is important [57]. Fig. 5b shows the variations in the recognition rate with the number of epochs for both the software-based numerical case and the Al/PMMA-fGQD/ITO-based experimental case. In the initial state, the recognition rates of the two cases are 9.92 and 9.88 %, respectively. When the number of epochs increases to 50, the recognition rates of the two cases become 93.02 and 89.61 %, respectively. The new values of the weight are updated in each epoch by using backpropagation and gradient-descent methods. The weight is optimized to the neural network via the gradient-descent method, i.e., the value of the weight is adjusted to minimize errors for the neural network [58]. After the values of the weight for the software-based neural network have been numerically updated by using gradient-descent backpropagation, the numerical values of the weight are quantized and normalized to the conductance (multi-level status) of real devices for the simulation of the hardware implementation. The kurtosis of the distribution for the quantized and normalized values of the weight increases with the increasing number of epochs, as shown in Fig. 5c. When the epoch number is 0, the weight distribution is uniformly dispersed over a wide range, and the kurtosis is 2.19. For the 1st, 5th, and 50th epochs, the corresponding values of the kurtosis are 2.63, 7.07, and 12.71, respectively. Thus the kurtosis at the 50th epoch has the largest value. Fig. 5d shows a weight map of numbers from 0 to 9 after the initial, 1st, 5th, and 50th epochs, respectively. When the digit pixel is outputted from the neural network during the training process, the weight assigned to the synapse gradually increases if the error value is small in comparison with the label. On the other hand, when the error value becomes large, the weight assigned to the synapse gradually decreases. Therefore, an intuitive image of the target number can be in the weight map. Fig. 5e shows a confusion matrix of the recognition rate for the MNIST dataset. The confusion matrix offers an intuitive understanding of the discrepancy between the inference result and the labeled output [59]. The confusion matrix can be visualized by using the brightness of each cell, with the brightness being normalized from 0 to 100 %. The initial recognition rate for each number is very low except for “0” and “9”, while the final recognition rates for all numbers have increased to at least 82.93 %.

In conclusion, we proposed a synaptic device based on a PMMA-fGQD nanocomposite and investigated the effect of the fGQD concentration on the electrical properties of that device. The device with an fGQD concentration of 5 wt% exhibited the best electrical characteristics. We also investigated various synaptic characteristics, such as LTP, LTD, PPF, STM, LTM, learning experience, and STDP, as well as yielding successful mimicking in synaptic functions. We evaluated the MNIST pattern recognition simulations based on the synaptic characteristics of the devices, and the simulation results show that the device achieved about an 89.61 % recognition rate in the 50th epoch, indicative of clear digital classification.

Seong Yeon Ryu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hyung Soon Kim: Project administration. Jun Seop An: Software. Youngjin Kim: Supervision. Haoqun An: Formal analysis. Jong-Ryeol Kim: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Kijung Yoon: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Tae Whan Kim: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. 2018R1A5A7025522).
